display_name: An incrementing EVPN MAC mobility sequence number means the host is moving, not that the table is corrupt
context: Inspecting EVPN Type-2 routes and seeing a non-zero or rising MAC Mobility extended community sequence number for a host MAC
observation: The MAC mobility sequence number for a MAC address is non-zero and increasing over time across the fabric
naive_interpretation: The EVPN table is corrupt, the route is unstable, or there is a software bug incrementing a counter
correct_interpretation: The sequence number is the EVPN mechanism for arbitrating where a MAC currently lives. Each time a MAC is detected behind a new leaf, the advertising leaf increments the sequence number so all leaves agree the highest sequence wins. A slowly rising number tracks legitimate host mobility (vMotion, failover). A rapidly incrementing number means the same MAC is bouncing between two leaves — a true flap, usually from a layer-2 loop, dual-homing misconfiguration, or a duplicated MAC. Watch the rate, not the value.
why: RFC 7432 Section 7.7 defines the MAC Mobility extended community and the sequence number used to resolve which advertisement is most recent
A higher sequence number always supersedes a lower one, so the number monotonically increases for the lifetime of that MAC's mobility — a non-zero value is normal after any move
RFC 7432 Section 15.1 defines duplicate-MAC detection: a default of N moves (commonly 5) within M seconds (commonly 180) flags the MAC as duplicate and can freeze advertisement
Distinguishing legitimate mobility from a flap requires the rate of change, not the absolute sequence value

confirmation_commands.purpose: Show the Type-2 route and its MAC mobility sequence number plus the originating leaf
List MAC entries with their sequence numbers and producing source to spot a MAC learned from two leaves
Look for duplicate-MAC or mac-move events that indicate a true flap versus a single legitimate move

traps: Treating any non-zero sequence number as an error — a non-zero value just means the host has moved at least once
Reacting to the absolute number instead of the rate of increase, which is what separates a flap from normal mobility
Not identifying the two leaves the MAC is bouncing between — the fix lives at the dual-homing or loop, not in EVPN
Assuming duplicate-MAC freeze is a bug when it is the protocol deliberately damping a flapping or duplicated address
